text:
A System Evaluation Study

This research project employs a design-based research approach. As the system advances in its development, an initial heuristic evaluation was conducted, and the findings are presented in this section. Heuristic evaluation

The cleanroom VR training program was reengineered from an earlier PC-based application to leverage the advantages of standalone VR technology. To assess its usability and instructional effectiveness, we conducted a heuristic evaluation with six domain experts representing diverse professional backgrounds.

Heuristic evaluation is a widely recognized usability inspection method that allows researchers and designers to identify interface issues based on established usability principles. Originally proposed by Jakob Nielsen and Rolf Molich [40], the method involves having a small group of evaluators systematically review a system using a set of heuristics such as feedback visibility, user control, error prevention, and consistency. Unlike user testing, which involves empirical observation, heuristic evaluation leverages expert judgment to uncover design flaws early in development.

This evaluation method is particularly useful for identifying usability problems in the early design stages or after major system revisions. It is cost-effective, does not require large-scale participant recruitment, and enables rapid feedback cycles. Each evaluator reviews the system independently and records observed usability issues along with severity ratings, which are later aggregated to form a comprehensive view of the system’s strengths and limitations.

For this study, all the evaluators independently interacted with the standalone VR simulation and completed a structured checklist designed in Excel. The original heuristic checklist consisted of 10 items; however, it lacked coverage of critical pedagogical dimensions such as instructional delivery and instructional goals, elements essential for evaluating interactive learning systems. To address this gap, we further adapted the heuristic items according to (citation) and aligned it with the specific affordances of the VR learning environment. As a result, three additional items targeted VR-specific pedagogical considerations were added, bringing the total to 13 heuristic items and 28 sub-items.

It is important to note that this represents the initial round of heuristic evaluation in our study; further iterations and refinements are planned as part of the project’s timeline.

Each item was rated by all evaluators, allowing for comprehensive coverage of usability concerns across the simulation. In addition to numerical severity ratings, evaluators provided qualitative feedback through open-ended comments. These insights offered contextual details about specific issues, clarified ambiguous scores, and provided targeted recommendations for improvement.

To better understand the frequency, impact, and persistence of the usability issues, the usability issues were rated using a five-point severity scale (citation):

0 – Not a usability problem

1 – Cosmetic issue

2 – Minor usability problem

3 – Major usability problem

4 – Usability catastrophe

Participant Demographics

A total of six evaluators participated in the heuristic evaluation, each bringing distinct academic or technical expertise relevant to the VR simulation. The participants were selected to ensure a multidisciplinary perspective, reflecting a range of insights from education, design, and engineering domains.

The group comprised two doctoral students from the Information Science and Learning Technologies program, who contributed expertise in instructional design and learning technologies. A faculty member from Engineering, with a background in virtual reality and game development, provided insights into system usability and technical fidelity. Two faculty members from the School of Visual Studies offered perspectives on interface design, visual communication, and user experience. The final participant, a doctoral student in Electrical and Computer Engineering who regularly works with cleanroom equipment replicated in the simulation, contributed expertise on procedural accuracy and alignment with actual laboratory practices. The diverse backgrounds of these participants, including virtual reality, engineering, visual design, and instructional technology, ensured a comprehensive and well-informed evaluation of the simulation from multiple disciplinary viewpoints.

Heuristic Evaluation Results

In the following sections, results of the heuristic evaluations are presented in three aspects: severity ratings overview, severity ratings by heuristic items, usability issues by heuristic items, and open-ended questions results.

Severity Rating Overview

These evaluators rated 28 sub-items distributed across 13 main heuristic items. This process resulted in a total of 168 severity scores. These ratings were grouped according to their assigned severity level, ranging from 0 to 4, to assess the overall usability performance of the simulation.

Figure? presents the proportional distribution of all ratings. This finding indicates substantial usability strength in the current design iteration, with 67.1% of items rated as having no usability issues (score 0), suggesting a solid baseline for further refinement.

Figure ?: Distribution of severity ratings from heuristic evaluation.

The remaining 32.9% of ratings were distributed across the other severity levels. Severity 1 (Cosmetic problem only) accounted for 4.2%, indicating some usability issues were minor and do not require an immediate fix. Examples of cosmetic problems include, the aesthetic design of the interface could be aligned with the design of the environment. Severity 2 (minor usability problem) accounted for 13.8%, reflecting minor

usability concerns that may cause some user hesitation but are unlikely to prevent task completion. Examples of minor usability problems include a lack of timely feedback on the operation performance. Severity 3 (major usability problem) made up 11.4%, pointing to more substantial issues that could disrupt user performance if not addressed. Examples of major usability problems include a lack of guided instructions to indicate what to do next, hence learners felt lost during the process. Severity 4 (usability catastrophe), though less frequent at 3.6%, reflected critical usability problems requiring immediate revision. Examples of usability catastrophe include inconsistent input response on the tablet. Meaning when touching the virtual tablet screen in VR, the system sometimes does not register the input immediately, requiring multiple taps before it responds.

Severity Ratings by Heuristic Items

To further assess usability across distinct aspects of the simulation, severity ratings were grouped by heuristic items. This approach enabled systematic identification and structuring of ratings, thereby supporting more effective iterative system refinement.

Figure ? displays the distribution of severity levels (0–4) across all 13 heuristic items. Each bar corresponds to the total number of ratings received per item, with the total based on the number of sub-items it contains. For example, a heuristic item with four sub- items evaluated by six participants would generate 24 ratings.

Figure ?: Severity level distribution across 13 heuristic evaluation categories.

Some categories, such as Learning and Consistency and Standards, demonstrated strong performance. Learning received 22 out of 24 ratings at Severity 0, suggesting that the simulation's instructional components were generally clear and well-integrated. Consistency and Standards also performed well, with most of its ratings falling within the Severity 0 to 1 range, indicating that design conventions and interface behavior met user expectations.

In contrast, categories like Visibility of System Status, Help and Documentation, and Comfort revealed more mixed results. Visibility of System Status, for example, showed a wide range of severity levels, including multiple ratings at Severity 2 and 3. Help and Documentation included four Severity 2 ratings and one Severity 4, suggesting gaps in user support or task guidance. Comfort received a combination of Severity 3 and 4 ratings, highlighting potential physical or environmental usability concerns within the VR experience.

These mixed severities across categories help identify not just gaps in system design but also patterns in participant perception. In several cases, even when fewer evaluators rated an issue with high severity, the nature of the concern was shared across individuals. For example, in the Comfort category, two participants assigned a Severity 4 rating, and both suggested the addition of alternative locomotion techniques. One evaluator explicitly noted, “Consider including different ways of locomotion into the VR environment.” While only two out of six participants flagged this issue, the agreement in their qualitative feedback and the high severity assigned suggest that it may significantly affect user comfort and navigation. Instances like these highlight the importance of considering both the severity level and the consistency of participant suggestions when prioritizing usability refinements.

Usability Issues by Percentages

Each severity rating refers to a design component that the evaluator observed and recorded. In this section, we present the percentages of usability issues regarding all the design components that the evaluators have identified and rated.

Table X. Usability Issues by Percentages

Heuristic Items Percentage (%) 1 Visibility of system status. 54.17% 2 Match Between System and the Real World. 50.00% 3 Consistency and standards. 16.67% 4 Error prevention. 50.00% 5 Recognition Rather Than Recall. 41.67% 6 Flexibility and Efficiency of Use. 33.33% 7 User Control and Freedom. 66.67% 8 Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors. 50.00% 9 Help and Documentation. 50.00% 10 Interaction 20.83% 11 Comfort 25.00% 12 Orientation and Navigation 25.00% 13 Learning 8.33%

According to Table X, 66.67% of the designs related to User Control and Freedom appear to have some level of usability concerns; this is followed by 54.17% on Visibility of System Status; 50% on Match Between System, The Real World and Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors, and Error Prevention, respectively. Next, 41.67% on Recognition Rather than Recall, 33.33% on Flexibility and Efficiency of Use, 25% on Comfort and Orientation and Navigation respectively, 20.83% on Interaction, 16.67% on Consistency and Standards, and lastly, 8.33% on Learning.

Usability Issues by Severity Ratings

Usability issues identified through the heuristic evaluation process were identified through the severity rates that were higher than rating score “0”, meaning “not a usability problem at all”. Hence, we presented the usability issues in the section below.

Table X. Usability Issues by Severity Ratings

Usability Issues /Severity Ratings Cosmetic Problem (1) Minor Usability issue (2) Major Usability Issue (3) Usability Catastrophe (4) SUM 1 Visibility of system status. 3 7 3 0 13 2 Match Between System and the Real World. 0 5 1 0 6 3 Consistency and standards. 1 1 0 0 2 4 Error prevention. 0 1 2 0 3 5 Recognition Rather Than Recall. 1 2 2 0 5 6 Flexibility and Efficiency of Use. 0 1 1 0 2 7 User Control and Freedom. 0 3 1 0 4 8 Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors. 0 2 1 0 3 9 Help and Documentation. 1 0 4 1 6 10 Interaction 0 2 2 1 5 11 Comfort 1 0 0 2 3 12 Orientation and Navigation 0 0 2 1 3 13 Learning 0 1 0 1 2 Sub-total 7 25 19 6 57

According to Table X, the majority of the usability issues were related to Visibility of System Status. Among the Visibility of System Status items (n=13), three usability issues were identified as major issues, 7 issues were considered minor problems, and 3 issues were considered cosmetic problems. Among the Match between System and the Real-World items (n=6), one usability issue was considered as major problem, and 5 issues were considered as minor problems. Similarly, among Help and Documentation items (n=6), one issue was rated as usability catastrophe, another issue was rated as cosmetic issue, and the last four items were rated as major usability problems.

Next, among the Interactions items (n=5), only one usability issue was considered as a catastrophe, 2 issues were considered major problems, and another 2 issues were

considered minor concerns. Similarly, in terms of the Recognition rather than Recall items (n=5), 2 issues were considered major; 2 issues were rated as minor; and the last issue was rated as a cosmetic problem.

Moving on, among the User Control and Freedom items (n=4), one issue was rated as a major concern, and the rest of four issues were considered minor. Among the Comfort Items (n=3), two issues were rated as usability catastrophes and one issue was considered a cosmetic problem. Similarly, for the Orientation and Navigation items (n=3), one usability issue was considered as a catastrophe, and the other two issues were rated as major issues.

In addition, three usability issues were related to Error Prevention (n=3), and another three usability issues were categorized as Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors (n=3). Among the Error Prevention items, two usability issues were considered as major concerns, while one issue was a minor issue. On the other hand, among the Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors items, there were two minor usability issues and one major issue.

Lastly, for the Learning items (n=2), one usability issue was rated as a usability catastrophe, and another issue was considered a minor problem. For the Consistency and Standards items (n=2), one issue was recognized as a cosmetic problem and another one was a minor concern. And for Flexibility and Efficiency of Use items, there was one minor usability issue and one major usability issue.

Usability Issues in Detail

During the evaluation, participants provided open-ended comments describing the usability challenges they encountered in the VR environment, along with suggestions for improving the current learning system.

Table X presents examples of identified usability issues and the corresponding recommendations from evaluators. For instance, one critical issue under the “Recognition Rather Than Recall” heuristic was the absence of sequential guidance throughout the learning process. Evaluators recommended the inclusion of visual highlights or prompts to help learners remain focused on the current task. Another prominent issue, categorized under “Visibility of System Status,” was the lack of sufficient and timely feedback. Without clear feedback, learners could easily overlook task completion status and remain uncertain about their progress. To address this, evaluators suggested the implementation of multimodal and timely system feedback to keep users oriented. A further issue, grouped under “Help and Documentation,” was the complete absence of supporting materials. Evaluators emphasized the importance

of integrating help documents or procedural protocols to assist learners when encountering difficulties. Additional minor usability issues and their associated recommendations are detailed in Table X below.

Table X. Usability Issues and Recommendations

Heuristic Items Examples Usability Issues Recommendations by Evaluators 1 Visibility of system status. Upon successful completion of a step, the system only provides visual feedback, which can sometimes be overlooked by users. This may lead to user uncertainty about whether the action was successfully completed, potentially disrupting the flow of interaction. The system should incorporate auditory feedback (in addition to visual feedback) in a timely manner, to strengthen users' perception and confirmation of their actions. 2 Match Between System and the Real World. The objects in VR space have similar interactions as the objects in the real world. However, the Mask Aligner seems oversized. The size of the devices and tools in the VR environment needs to be resized.

3 Consistency and standards. Hand interactions are consistent throughout the experience, and the knobs generally feel natural to grasp. However, additional hand poses would improve realism, particularly for the knobs on the Mask Aligner. The handle on the spin coater lid, in contrast, lacks a realistic feel. There is a need to add a few more hand poses to the knobs of the mask aligner, so that it looks more consistent with real-world experience 4 Error prevention. There is no conformation message on the hot plate device, or the mask aligner machine. Hence, the user cannot avoid any error after starting the device. Before inputting the parameters into the machine, it is ideal to implement the confirmation page before launching the operations. 5 Recognition Rather Than Recall. Lack of sequential guidance for operations on UV light interface During the operation, sequentially highlighting the corresponding buttons would help users better understand the correct order of actions.

6 Flexibility and Efficiency of Use. There are some items irrelevant in terms of interactions with the UI, such as the Lab Wipers. It is not an interactable object; it is just a decoration but put in a prominent place. Remove irrelevant items in the environment in the case of confusions 7 User Control and Freedom. When users get stuck during a particular step, the system lacks appropriate support mechanisms. There is no easily accessible "exit," "reset," or "help" option, leaving users uncertain about how to recover, seek assistance, or proceed with the task. Implement easily accessible "Exit," "Reset," and "Help" options throughout the experience. These should be consistently available via the main menu or a quick-access interface, allowing users to exit the task, restart a step, or request contextual guidance when they encounter difficulties.

8 Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors. There is no recovery function available in the VR. This means the user cannot easily "cancel" or "recover" when they make some mistakes. Consider implementing a "Restart Task” function that allows users to manually restart the current mission at any time. This will help users recover smoothly if they encounter errors or become stuck during the task. 9 Help and Documentation. There is no help document in the environment. There is no option to review tutorials or documentation. Need to include a help document, or an operation protocol for the XR cleanroom, meanwhile, ensure they are being placed in a prominent place. 10 Interaction Currently, interactive objects are highlighted with a red outline. However, for smaller items such as buttons, this method is not sufficiently noticeable. Enhance the visibility of smaller interactive objects by combining multiple highlighting techniques. In addition to a red outline, consider using effects such as dynamic glow, size pulsing, or subtle motion cues (e.g., slight bouncing or shimmering).

11 Comfort Some knob-based operations, such as adjusting to a specific temperature or aligning with a target, are difficult to perform accurately in VR. It may be helpful to simplify the VR interaction, for example by automatically snapping to the correct position once the user reaches a nearby range. 12 Orientation and Navigation The current VR application does not provide different locomotion methods. It is recommended to offer more natural locomotion options, such as simulated walking or teleportation. 13 Learning Users can follow the hints system to complete the operation. However, there is no progress bar to indicate the progress of the operations. Users might not know where they are at in terms of the training. It would be helpful to include the progress bar into the VR application, so that learners can visually see the progress of their learning.

Note: In the usability issues columns, the issue colored in RED means usability catastrophe; the issue colored in ORANGE means major usability problems; and the issue colored in YELLOW means minor usability problems.

User eye tracking data

Through the heuristic evaluation process, we collected and analyzed user interaction and eye-tracking data. This analysis validated the reliability of the data logging feature and revealed several system design issues that informed subsequent refinements.

3

Figure ?: Eye tracking heatmap.

Fig. ? illustrates a 3D heatmap of a user’s gaze during the heuristic evaluation. The plot shows the spatial distribution of gaze focus points in three dimensions, along with the density, representing the duration of visual attention. The heatmap highlights three major pieces of equipment in the photolithography room: the hot plates, the spinner, and the mask aligner. Beyond visualization, the 3D heatmap offers valuable insights for future applications. Specifically, the data can be leveraged to optimize workflow and signage design and placement, detect whether learners spend excessive time (indicating confusion) or insufficient time (indicating oversight) on critical steps, or establish baselines of expert versus novice gaze behavior to support the development of objective performance metrics.

Introducing Interactive Learning Hub

The results from the first round of heuristic evaluation indicate that clear, well-structured, and timely instructional content is critical for students to effectively engage with new materials. In response, this paper proposes an innovative design to support learning in a VR training environment. The following section outlines the iterative design improvements implemented and presents the most recent version of the system.

Design Improvements

A central innovation was the development of the Interactive Learning Hub, which now provides multimodal learning experience (e.g., auditory signals, step-by-step guided instruction, a learning progress bar, quiz,). This Hub also integrates sequential prompts, help documentation, and flexible navigation tools, creating a consistent and accessible support system throughout the training experience.

The system was further modernized to improve realism and interaction fidelity. All equipment was 3D scanned using photogrammetry to ensure true-to-size accuracy, while new hand poses were implemented to replicate authentic operational gestures. Interactions with subtle objects, such as knobs and buttons, were optimized to improve comfort and precision.

Enhancements were also made to efficiency and error management. Redundant or non-interactable items were removed to minimize distractions, and confirmation steps were

added to prevent errors when operating devices. A reset function was incorporated, giving learners greater control and freedom to recover from mistakes or restart tasks when needed.

Navigation and mobility were expanded by adding both continuous movement and teleportation options, accessible directly through the Learning Hub, ensuring users can choose their preferred method of locomotion. In addition, the inclusion of a progress-tracking bar now provides learners with real-time visibility into their advancement through the training. See Table X for the detailed improvements we have completed.

Table X. Iterative Design Improvement

Heuristic Items Examples Usability Issues Design Improvement 1 Visibility of system status. Upon successful completion of a step, the system only provides visual feedback, which can sometimes be overlooked by users. This may lead to user uncertainty about whether the action was successfully completed, potentially disrupting the flow of interaction. We have created a brand-new Interactive Learning Hub in the VR system, incorporated multimodal feedback and the progress of their operations, where users will be informed the operation status when they have any interactions with the system (e.g., auditory sound, green highlight and red highlight, progress bar) 2 Match Between System and the Real World. The objects in VR space have similar interactions as the objects in the real world. However, the Mask Aligner seems oversized. All the devices and tools were being 3D scanned using the photogrammetry technique to ensure they are true to size.

3 Consistency and standards. Hand interactions are consistent throughout the experience, and the knobs generally feel natural to grasp. However, additional hand poses would improve realism, particularly for the knobs on the Mask Aligner. The handle on the spin coater lid, in contrast, lacks a realistic feel. A set of new hand poses were created and implemented into the new VR environment. These hand poses were simulated based on real-human hand interactions in the real operation scenario (e.g., turning the knob of the machine) 4 Error prevention. There is no conformation message on the hot plate device, or the mask aligner machine. Hence, the user cannot avoid any error after starting the device. We have further improved the UI interaction flow of all the devices; users can now confirm their intention of the interaction before the actual operations of the system. 5 Recognition Rather Than Recall. Lack of sequential guidance for operations on UV light interface We have significantly improved the instructional guidance in the VR system. We have created a brand-new feature, The Interactive Learning Hub, where all the guided prompts are implemented.

6 Flexibility and Efficiency of Use. There are some items irrelevant in terms of interactions with the UI, such as the Lab Wipers. It is not an interactable object; it is just a decoration but put in a prominent place. In the latest VR environment, irrelevant objects and redundant designs have been eliminated, ensuring learners’ attentions are focused on the task. 7 User Control and Freedom. When users get stuck during a particular step, the system lacks appropriate support mechanisms. There is no easily accessible "exit," "reset," or "help" option, leaving users uncertain about how to recover, seek assistance, or proceed with the task. A reset function has been implemented into the VR application where learners can press down the Home button and restart the experience. 8 Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors. There is no recovery function available in the VR. This means the user cannot easily "cancel" or "recover" when they make some mistakes.

9 Help and Documentation. There is no help document in the environment. There is no option to review tutorials or documentation. We have implemented a Help Page to the Interactive Learning Hub, where learners can easily view the instructions of operations. 10 Interaction Interactive objects are highlighted with a red outline. However, for smaller items such as buttons, this method is not sufficiently noticeable. In the current VR system, interactive objects are not only highlighted but also provided with clear and timely guided prompt to help learners practice through the process. 11 Comfort Some knob-based operations, such as adjusting to a specific temperature or aligning with a target, are difficult to perform accurately in VR. Interactions with small and subtle objects in the VR has been optimized to ensure the experience is usable.

12 Orientation and Navigation The current VR application does not provide different locomotion methods. Two major movement methods are not incorporated into the VR – one is continuous movement, another one is teleportation. Learners can choose their preferred movement technique to move around in the environment. This feature is also accessible through the Interactive Learning Hub. 13 Learning Users can follow the hints system to complete the operation. However, there is no progress bar to indicate the progress of the operations. Users might not know where they are at in terms of the training. A progress bar function is now added to the Interactive Learning Hub, where learners can clearly view the learning progress on the top of the Hub UI.

Interactive Learning Hub Design

Building on the need for clear, structured, and timely instructional and interactive delivery identified in the heuristic evaluation, our latest VR modules, the photolithography module and the RIE module, adopt a consistent instructional sequence. Each section begins with a tutorial video, followed by a quiz question, and concludes with guided step-by-step practice to complete the tasks introduced in the video. In the project version tested through heuristic evaluations, this formula was implemented using TVs to convey the current instructions and a handheld tablet where the screen space was entirely consumed by this learning content (see figure x). In response to the growing need for additional quality of life features, this design was overhauled in favor of a more modernized and ever-present alternative referred to as the “Info-Center”.

Figure ?: Side-by-side comparison of the old instructional implementation using wall-hung TVs and a handheld tablet to guide the simulation versus the newly proposed Info-Center.

This Info-Center is best characterized as an interactive learning hub that is aestheticized to provide the user with a sense of direction and a notion of progress. Possessing a dark sheer background framed by a solid border, the design creates a futuristic ambiance and allows the user to still see parts of their environment throughout their engagement that would otherwise remain obscured. Multiple hand interaction snap points were also added so that the user can grab and adjust the panel location wherever they wish relative to themselves, as it follows them around by virtue of being attached to their player rig.

At the top of the panel, there is a “Process” label which indicates the name of the section the user is currently on, as well as a segmented progress bar for section progression. For photolithography, there are nine visible segments, and for RIE, there are eight. At the very bottom of the panel, there is a hotbar, another progress bar

expressed in terms of the module’s total steps, and indicators for session score and time elapsed. These displays are always visible.

Additionally, the Info-Center consists of three primary windows available at any time. The user begins the simulation training on the information panel denoted by an i symbol. There, they can scroll through descriptions of each of the Info-Center windows, as well as the key bindings and their purpose throughout the simulation. The information tab also refers to the toggle button found on the far right of the window hotbar, which can be used to toggle between smooth locomotion and locomotion consisting of teleportation and snap turning.

Figure ?: Three primary windows of the Info-Center including (from left to right) the information panel, AI panel, and step panel.

The second window is for the integrated AI assistant named NOVA. The AI’s iconography is reminiscent of a microchip and a human brain fused into one image to visually represent human-machine interaction and mental focus on the training content. When visiting this tab, the user may press the A button on their controller to activate NOVA’s listening capability. During this time, the logo is animated as if the microchip is intercepting soundwaves. Eight seconds are allotted for the user to speak a process-related question uninterrupted. When finished, the microchip border of the logo performs a loading animation while NOVA transcribes and processes the prompt. Finally, soundwaves are emitted from NOVA as the response is simultaneously written and spoken to the user. The tab clears conversation content on re-entry but retains a history button that allows them to return to a previous conversation if needed.

The final window is the step panel signified by a checklist icon. Here is where the primary educational content is displayed. On tutorial steps of the training, the area directly behind where the video is displayed becomes opaque for visual clarity. While watching the video users are given controls to pause, play, restart, or go back ten seconds before pressing the next arrow to continue to the quiz question.

Each section of the selected module has one unique quiz question. Some possess a related image, and some do not. Answer lengths and quantities vary. The design system is configured to accept these variations in a uniform manner. The user is required to

answer the question correctly before proceeding to the next step and is provided real-time feedback. When an answer is incorrect, a negative sound effect is instantly played, and the border of the Info-Center and selected answer flash red to draw user attention to the score point deduction. Alternatively, when an answer is correct, a positive sound effect is played to reinforce the idea and the border of the Info-Center and selected answer flash green accompanied by a point increase. There is a short delay for a satisfying effect once the correct answer is selected and the user is provided with an explanation for the answer. After this, the user walks through the same steps presented in the tutorial video by exploring the VR environment at a self-guided pace.

Figure ?: Overview of tutorial step flow where the user first watches a tutorial video (far left), then completes a quiz (middle is feedback for a correct answer, far right is feedback for an incorrect answer).

On steps not containing tutorial content, the user retains the ability to minimize or maximize the Info-Center using the key-bind B. When minimized, the Info-Center temporarily displays the current step instruction, the progress bar, time elapsed, and score before fading away after a few seconds. This “on-the-fly" preference option allows the user to streamline their progression through the steps, as the minimized Info-Center will automatically pop up with the next instruction once the previous step is completed. Positive and negative feedback are still enforced with the minimized version using the same green or red coloration response.

Users who choose to take advantage of the minimized Info-Center will have their attention immediately drawn to the maximized version once they reach a tutorial step at the same point when the scene lighting is dimmed. This functionality reduces potential confusion, as they must complete the video and quiz before progressing. After this, they are free to swap back to the minimized version.

Figure ?: Interchangeability of the maximized (left) Info-Center and minimized (right) Info-Center on demand for non-tutorial steps.

Certain modules may implement additional accessibility features present on particular process steps. For example, in the RIE module, a pop-up panel is added to the side of the Info-Center so that the user can more easily view the turbo pump and machine controller temperatures displayed on the screen of the RIE machine. Steps where this is necessary force-populate the maximized version, similar to any given tutorial step. This helps to prevent eye strain and progression difficulty while still emphasizing where to obtain the information on the machine in a real-world lab setting.

Figure ?: Side panel popup for the maximized Info-Center showing a closeup of the controller screen of the RIE machine.

This modern, user-friendly panel layout makes use of intuitive visual icons and color psychology to quickly convey key information to the user. The Info-Center provides a self-paced, yet structured user experience accompanied by an AI assistant to construct a supportive virtual learning environment
